Gates joined the top 10 on Forbes magazine’s billionaires list alongside Japanese, German, Canadian, South Korean and Swedish billionaires, including those with family fortunes from Britain and America. A broad mix of industries was on the list: Retail and media, property management and packaging, an investment firm and a couple of industrial conglomerates. Their fortunes almost added up to $100bn – equivalent to about 0.4% of the US’s GDP that year. The oligarchy has changed drastically since then. Bernard Arnault, of French luxury group LVMH, Amancio Ortega, the Spanish clothing mogul, and Warren Buffett, the US investor, were the only old-school billionaires among the top 10 in 2025. The rest largely made their money from high-tech: Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos, Mark Zuckerberg, Larry Ellison, Steve Ballmer and Google’s Sergey Brin and Larry Page. The top 10 amassed over $16trn, which is about 8% of US GDP. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Why are respected film-makers suddenly embracing AI?
source_signals.summary: From Soderbergh to Aronofsky, esteemed Hollywood directors are starting to find ways to include artificial intelligence in the production of their films In Steven Soderbergh’s beguiling new movie The Christophers , a reclusive artist (Ian McKellen) tangles with the quiet art forger (Michaela Coel) who his greedy children have hired to secretly finish further entries in a well-known painting series. The movie is smart and provocative about the nature of artistry and authorship, exploring what it means to create – and to stop creating. It’s especially fascinating coming from Soderbergh, who has made movies with workhorse dependability (The Christophers is his third theatrical release of the past 18 months) and also spent four years retired from directing features entirely. It also provides particularly jarring context for Soderbergh, in interviews promoting the film, to voice his interest in something that a lot of great artists have pointedly refused to embrace: using AI in films. Soderbergh mentioned in an interview with Filmmaker Magazine that he used what sounds like generative AI to produce “thematically surreal images that occupy a dream space rather than a literal space” for his upcoming documentary about John Lennon and Yoko Ono. He also said that a movie he’s hoping to make about the Spanish-American war would use “a lot of AI”. In a subsequent conversation with Variety, Soderbergh didn’t sound like an AI evangelist, but nor did he back down: “I don’t think it’s the solution to everything, and I don’t think it’s the death of everything. We’re in the very early stages. Five years from now, we all may be going, ‘That was a fun phase.’ We may end up not using it as much as we thought we were going to.” Continue reading...
numeric_hints: It’s especially fascinating coming from Soderbergh, who has made movies with workhorse dependability (The Christophers is his third theatrical release of the past 18 months) and also spent four years retired from directing features entirely.

source_signals.title: Fernando Mendoza: LinkedIn lunatic, endearing goofball and a symbol of the NFL’s future
source_signals.summary: The Indiana quarterback will almost certainly be the No overall pick in this week’s draft. He is also a sign of what’s to come for the league Ever since the NCAA changed its rules in 2021 to allow student athletes to profit from their name, image and likeness, institutional money has been circling college football, desperate for a way to turn top programs into money-making machines. These incursions have had limited success to date . Perhaps the private equity and venture capital vultures have been approaching things the wrong way. Instead of building a fully financialized and business-friendly college football league, why not start with the players? When Fernando Mendoza emerges, as he almost certainly will, as the No 1 overall pick of the NFL draft on Thursday night, his coronation will not only cap a remarkable personal story – it will also mark the ascendancy of a specific idea of the modern football player. Mendoza’s story is extraordinary. Ranked as the 140th-best quarterback prospect by respected college recruiting website 247Sports in 2022, as he was applying for college, he rose through the ranks at the California Golden Bears, earning both his stripes as a starting quarterback and an undergraduate business degree in three years. Last year he transferred to Indiana, winning the Heisman Trophy as he led the Hoosiers to an undefeated season and the national championship . His rise is a tribute to dedication, hard work, grit, determination – all qualities NFL franchises look for when scouring the college field for prospects. Continue reading...
numeric_hints: He is also a sign of what’s to come for the league Ever since the NCAA changed its rules in 2021 to allow student athletes to profit from their name, image and likeness, institutional money has been circling college football, desperate for a way to turn top programs into money-making machines.

When Fernando Mendoza emerges, as he almost certainly will, as the No 1 overall pick of the NFL draft on Thursday night, his coronation will not only cap a remarkable personal story – it will also mark the ascendancy of a specific idea of the modern football player.

Ranked as the 140th-best quarterback prospect by respected college recruiting website 247Sports in 2022, as he was applying for college, he rose through the ranks at the California Golden Bears, earning both his stripes as a starting quarterback and an undergraduate business degree in three years.

source_signals.title: ‘Take in the moments or they just pass you by‘: Aden Durde, the first British coach to win a Super Bowl
source_signals.summary: The Seattle Seahawks defensive coordinator is preparing for the NFL draft later this week. On a recent trip home to London, he reflected on his extraordinary journey A middle-aged man pulls down his baseball cap, walks across Leicester Square and heads to Greggs for lunch before taking the Piccadilly Line home to Southgate. It’s only two months since he won the Super Bowl but none of the thousands of tourists milling around central London recognise him. Aden Durde should be a British celebrity. Olympians often say there is a massive comedown after they win gold medals. Some think: ‘Now what?’ How have you felt after winning the Super Bowl? “I wouldn’t say it’s a comedown, but there were moments after you win it, like at the parade, I felt numb. The little letdown is, while you might get another chance to create it, you’re not going to do it again with that group of people. You realise that this special thing that we had is over. I thought that on the bus going back to the hotel from the game.” Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Gianluigi Buffon: ‘You have a perception that you are unbeatable, almost omnipotent’
source_signals.summary: Italy’s legendary goalkeeper on getting used to retirement, the decline of Italian football and why he blames himself for Zidane’s World Cup final red card “I tear the gloves off my hands and my bare knuckles, reddened and soaked with sweat, shine in the neon light,” Gianluigi Buffon writes when he remembers leaving the pitch at half-time during the final game of his remarkable career, in May 2023. “I really feel dead inside. I am 45 years old, and around me many of my teammates walking in shorts towards the dressing room could easily be my children.” The gripping and intimate tone of Buffon’s book, Saved , which opens with his last-ever game in a Serie B playoff for Parma, is matched by his warm and open character. The great goalkeeper played professionally for 28 years and his reflections are as moving as they are sombre. “Can you live without it, Gigi?” he asks. “No, I can’t … when you have outlived your youth, and the time when you feel strong and all-powerful has ended, and your muscles, joints and reflexes start to wear out, then it really is like dying.” Continue reading...

source_signals.summary: The ICC chair Jay Shah has dominated social media posts from global tournaments but his views on the future direction of the game are harder to find “Visuals that the whole nation will remember for ages,” reads the social media post by the Board of Control for Cricket in India, introducing a 37-second video clip. It captures the celebrations after India’s T20 World Cup triumph in Ahmedabad last month, a victory built by an astounding squad ; strong enough to exclude names such as Shubman Gill and Yashasvi Jaiswal, nerveless enough to thump 255 in the final against New Zealand. But one man in a suit dominates the footage: Jay Shah. The chair of the International Cricket Council (ICC) is in most of the shots, embracing the players and soaking in the moment alongside their head coach, Gautam Gambhir. He is all smiles alongside former India captains Rohit Sharma and MS Dhoni, and at one point there is a frame of him on his own holding the trophy. He goes on to help Suryakumar Yadav lift it. Shah is not the honorary secretary of the BCCI any more; the whole sport is his responsibility. Yet here he was, transformed into the protagonist of a story that did not belong to him. This is an extract from the Guardian’s weekly cricket email, The Spin. To subscribe, just visit this page and follow the instructions Continue reading...

at_a_glance: Iran war pressure continues to ripple through diplomacy, trade and household bills.
UK inflation rises to 3.3% as fuel costs feed through to transport and food prices.
EU approves a €90bn loan for Ukraine amid fresh signs of movement on the Druzhba pipeline.
Apple confirms a generational leadership change as John Ternus prepares to succeed Tim Cook.
Chelsea’s collapse and a string of transfer stories dominate a narrow football-heavy sport slate.
Markets remain exposed to oil volatility, with airlines and consumer prices already reacting.
The newsroom’s main international focus should stay on the Iran war’s economic and diplomatic spillovers.

lead_story.title: Trump buys time for Iran deal after frantic day of diplomacy
lead_story.summary: Donald Trump’s decision to hold back escalation after a frantic day of diplomacy gives Iran deal talks more time, but leaves the US, Israel and Iran facing continued uncertainty over the next step in a war already shaking oil markets, airline schedules and inflation forecasts.
lead_story.source_signal_title: Trump buys time for Iran deal after frantic day of diplomacy
body: Donald Trump’s decision to hold off on escalation has bought more time for diplomacy around Iran, but it has not reduced the sense of instability surrounding the war.
The conflict continues to move markets and planning far beyond the battlefield, with oil traders, airlines and governments all trying to judge whether the latest pause will hold.

That makes the moment important even without a dramatic breakthrough: every sign of restraint can ease pressure on fuel prices and aviation, while every reversal can quickly feed through to inflation and transport costs.
The problem for policymakers is that the situation remains fluid, and the gap between a temporary pause and a durable settlement is still very wide.

stories.title: Trump buys time for Iran deal after frantic day of diplomacy
stories.summary: Donald Trump’s pause on escalation extends the diplomatic opening around Iran, but also underlines how quickly the conflict between the US, Israel and Iran continues to move energy prices, aviation plans and regional security.
stories.source_signal_title: Trump buys time for Iran deal after frantic day of diplomacy
body: Donald Trump’s decision to hold off on escalation has bought more time for diplomacy around Iran, but it has not reduced the sense of instability surrounding the war.
The conflict continues to move markets and planning far beyond the battlefield, with oil traders, airlines and governments all trying to judge whether the latest pause will hold.

That makes the moment important even without a dramatic breakthrough: every sign of restraint can ease pressure on fuel prices and aviation, while every reversal can quickly feed through to inflation and transport costs.
The problem for policymakers is that the situation remains fluid, and the gap between a temporary pause and a durable settlement is still very wide.

stories.title: EU approves €90bn loan for Ukraine as pipeline is turned on ending deadlock
stories.summary: The European Union has approved a €90bn loan for Ukraine as Kyiv says it has reopened the Druzhba pipeline, ending a months-long stand-off over oil supplies to Hungary and easing one point of friction inside the bloc.
stories.source_signal_title: EU approves €90bn loan for Ukraine as pipeline is turned on ending deadlock
body: The European Union has approved a €90bn loan for Ukraine, while Kyiv says it has reopened the Druzhba pipeline in a move that appears to end a months-long dispute over oil supplies to Hungary.

The package is significant because financial support remains central to Ukraine’s war effort and its ability to keep government services running, while energy disputes inside the bloc have repeatedly exposed political tensions between member states.

The reopening of the pipeline matters too: Druzhba has been a focal point of friction over Russian oil flows, and the stalemate over supplies to Hungary had become one more source of division inside the EU.
Together, the two developments suggest a partial easing of deadlock on both the financial and energy fronts, though neither issue is fully resolved.

Ukraine and its partners still face the larger challenge of sustaining support as the war drags on, and Hungary’s energy dependence will continue to shape negotiations over future sanctions and supply arrangements.

stories.title: Lufthansa cuts 20,000 summer flights as fuel prices surge
stories.summary: Lufthansa is cutting 20,000 summer flights as surging fuel prices linked to the Iran war force airlines to adjust schedules and protect margins across Europe.
stories.source_signal_title: Lufthansa cuts 20,000 summer flights as fuel prices surge
body: Lufthansa is cutting 20,000 summer flights as fuel prices rise sharply in the wake of the Iran war, forcing airlines across Europe to rethink schedules and protect margins.
The German carrier is the latest to trim capacity as jet fuel costs climb, a reminder that conflict in the Middle East is feeding directly into the aviation industry’s cost base.

For passengers, the immediate risk is fewer options and higher fares during the busy summer season; for airlines, the issue is whether they can keep flying profitably if fuel stays expensive and routes remain disrupted.
These decisions are also being driven by uncertainty: carriers do not yet know how long the conflict will affect airspace, shipping and refinery supply chains.
That makes the summer outlook unusually difficult to read, especially for a sector that has only recently returned to steadier demand after years of shocks.

stories.title: Zelensky says failure of US envoys to visit Kyiv is 'disrespectful'
stories.summary: Volodymyr Zelensky has criticised the fact that US envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner have travelled to Moscow several times but never to Kyiv, a contrast that underscores the diplomatic imbalance around the war settlement talks.
stories.source_signal_title: Zelensky says failure of US envoys to visit Kyiv is 'disrespectful'
body: Volodymyr Zelensky has criticised the fact that US envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner have travelled to Moscow several times but never to Kyiv, sharpening a diplomatic row over the shape of war settlement talks.

The Ukrainian president’s complaint highlights a familiar concern in Kyiv: that negotiations over Ukraine’s future can be shaped by outsiders without enough direct engagement with the country most affected.
The contrast matters because visits are often read as a sign of where priorities lie, and repeated trips to Moscow without a reciprocal stop in Kyiv can fuel suspicions about imbalance in the talks.
The broader backdrop is a war in which diplomatic channels remain active but fragile, with no clear sign that either Moscow or Kyiv is ready to accept terms the other would consider acceptable.
For now, the dispute is as much about symbolism and respect as it is about substance, but such signals can still influence the atmosphere around any eventual settlement.

stories.title: Mass trial for 486 alleged gang members begins in El Salvador
stories.summary: A court in El Salvador has begun the mass trial of 486 alleged gang members, a case that highlights President Nayib Bukele’s hard-line approach and renewed human-rights concerns over due process.
stories.source_signal_title: Mass trial for 486 alleged gang members begins in El Salvador
body: A court in El Salvador has begun a mass trial of 486 alleged gang members, in one of the clearest examples yet of President Nayib Bukele’s hard-line campaign against gang violence.

The case has drawn renewed concern from human-rights groups because collective proceedings on this scale raise questions about due process, access to legal representation and the ability of defendants to answer individual allegations properly.
Footage released by the attorney general’s office shows large groups of men in prison attending the hearing by video link, underlining the extraordinary scale of the prosecution.

Supporters of Mr Bukele argue that his emergency powers have restored public order in a country once terrorised by gangs; critics say the crackdown has come at the expense of legal safeguards and fair trials.
What happens next will be watched closely both in El Salvador and abroad, because the outcome may shape how far governments elsewhere are willing to go in the name of security.

stories.title: World update 6
stories.summary: A significant development in world with wider consequences under review.
stories.source_signal_title: Trump buys time for Iran deal after frantic day of diplomacy
body: Donald Trump’s decision to hold back escalation in the Iran conflict has given diplomacy more time, but the wider effects of the war are still being felt across energy markets, airlines and inflation forecasts.
The pause has eased immediate fears of a sharper military turn, yet it also leaves the next move uncertain and the region exposed to rapid change.
For households and businesses far from the fighting, the consequences are already visible in higher fuel costs and disrupted travel plans.
Much now depends on whether talks can keep pace with events on the ground, or whether a fragile ceasefire gives way to another round of confrontation.

stories.title: UK inflation rises to 3.3% amid biggest jump in fuel prices in more than three years
stories.summary: The Office for National Statistics says UK inflation rose to 3.3% in March as the Iran war pushed up fuel costs, with food and air fares also rising and the pressure now feeding into the cost of living debate.
stories.source_signal_title: UK inflation rises to 3.3% amid biggest jump in fuel prices in more than three years
body: UK inflation rose to 3.3% in March, according to the Office for National Statistics, after the Iran war pushed fuel prices up by the biggest amount in more than three years.
The figures are the first official sign of how the conflict is feeding into the cost of living in Britain, with food and air fares also moving higher.

That matters because fuel costs often feed quickly into broader household spending, from transport to freight to supermarket bills, and they can complicate the Bank of England’s choices on interest rates.
The March reading was up from 3% in February and matched City expectations, but it still reinforces pressure on families already facing high prices in several parts of the economy.
If energy markets remain volatile, the inflation picture could worsen again in coming months, adding to political arguments over living standards and economic resilience.

stories.title: Oil prices fluctuate as Trump extends Iran war ceasefire
stories.summary: Oil markets are swinging as Donald Trump extends the Iran war ceasefire, leaving traders to assess whether the latest pause will hold and whether shipping disruption can be contained.
stories.source_signal_title: Oil prices fluctuate as Trump extends Iran war ceasefire
body: Oil prices have been fluctuating after Donald Trump said he was extending the Iran war ceasefire, leaving traders to judge whether the latest pause will hold.
The president also said the US would continue to blockade Iran’s ports until peace talks progress, a sign that pressure on Tehran is still being used alongside diplomacy.
Markets are reacting to a familiar problem: even a temporary easing in the fighting can move prices sharply, but any sign of renewed disruption to shipping can send them back up just as fast.
For consumers and businesses, the issue is not only the level of oil prices but the uncertainty itself, which makes budgeting for transport, fuel and goods more difficult.
The next phase will depend on whether ceasefire terms are honoured and whether the shipping routes most exposed to the conflict remain open.

stories.title: Air fares soar by nearly 25% as Iran war forces flights to re-route
stories.summary: Air fares have risen by nearly 25% as airlines reroute around conflict-related airspace restrictions, adding another channel through which the Iran war is hitting households and business travel.
stories.source_signal_title: Air fares soar by nearly 25% as Iran war forces flights to re-route
body: Air fares have risen by nearly 25% as airlines reroute flights around conflict-related airspace restrictions linked to the Iran war.

The rise shows how quickly a regional conflict can hit ordinary travellers, not only through higher ticket prices but also through longer journeys, altered schedules and reduced availability on some routes.

For airlines, rerouting can mean extra fuel burn and higher operating costs at a time when margins are already under strain, which increases the chance that more of the cost is passed on to passengers.
That leaves both leisure and business travel exposed, especially if restrictions continue and carriers are forced to keep adapting their networks.
The broader risk is that aviation inflation becomes another channel through which the war reaches household budgets, alongside fuel, food and freight.

stories.title: World's biggest condom maker set to raise prices due to Iran war
stories.summary: Malaysia’s Karex, the world’s biggest condom maker, says it may raise prices as freight costs and supply-chain disruption linked to the Iran war continue to squeeze margins.
stories.source_signal_title: World's biggest condom maker set to raise prices due to Iran war
body: Karex, the world’s biggest condom maker, says it may raise prices as the Iran war continues to push up freight costs and disrupt supply chains.

The Malaysia-based company produces more than five billion condoms a year and supplies major global brands such as Durex and Trojan, so any price change could feed through to retailers in multiple markets.

Its warning is a reminder that war-driven cost pressures do not stop at obvious sectors like fuel and aviation; they can also affect consumer goods through shipping delays, higher insurance costs and tighter inventories.
For buyers, the impact may be modest on any single purchase, but it adds to a wider pattern of inflationary pressure that is becoming harder for companies to absorb.
How far prices rise will depend on how long logistics remain strained and whether the conflict continues to disrupt regional trade routes.

stories.title: Car finance compensation scheme faces challenge and delay
stories.summary: The car finance compensation scheme faces fresh legal challenge and delay, raising the prospect that payouts to millions of mis-sold drivers will take longer than expected.
stories.source_signal_title: Car finance compensation scheme faces challenge and delay
body: The car finance compensation scheme faces a fresh legal challenge, raising the prospect that payouts to millions of mis-sold drivers will be delayed again.

The dispute matters because it affects one of the largest consumer redress exercises in recent UK financial history, with borrowers already waiting years to learn whether they will receive compensation.
Any further delay will frustrate motorists who believe they were charged unfairly, and it could also complicate the Financial Conduct Authority’s attempts to bring clarity to the market.
The uncertainty reflects how difficult it can be to unwind past mis-selling on a system-wide scale, especially when lenders, lawyers and regulators disagree over the rules for repayment.
For consumers, the next step is likely to be more waiting, even as the legal and regulatory arguments continue.

stories.title: Apple names new boss to replace Tim Cook after 15 years
stories.summary: Apple has confirmed John Ternus will succeed Tim Cook in September after 15 years at the helm, marking a major transition at the company as it confronts pressure in AI and hardware.
stories.source_signal_title: Apple names new boss to replace Tim Cook after 15 years
body: Apple has confirmed that John Ternus will succeed Tim Cook in September, ending a 15-year era under the chief executive who turned the company into one of the world’s most valuable businesses.

The transition is important because Apple is entering a period of pressure in both AI and hardware, with investors watching whether the company can stay ahead in new product cycles while remaining as commercially disciplined as it has been under Cook.
Ternus, who has led hardware engineering, is seen as a product-focused insider rather than a radical outsider, which may reassure staff and markets looking for continuity.
But the handover also raises questions about whether Apple can broaden beyond the iPhone, sharpen its response to rivals in artificial intelligence and keep its huge installed base engaged.
The next few months will be watched for signs of how much strategic change the new chief is prepared to make, and how much of Cook’s model he intends to preserve.

stories.title: How will Apple change under 'product guy' John Ternus?
stories.summary: John Ternus’s rise to Apple’s top job raises questions about whether the company can broaden beyond the iPhone, sharpen its AI response and maintain the scale that Tim Cook delivered.
stories.source_signal_title: How will Apple change under 'product guy' John Ternus?
body: John Ternus’s rise to Apple’s top job raises a simple question: can a long-serving product executive steer the company through its next phase of growth?
Apple’s challenge is not just replacing Tim Cook, but deciding whether to push harder into AI, reduce dependence on the iPhone and open new areas of hardware and services.
That matters because the company’s scale can make change difficult even when the need is obvious; the market wants innovation, but it also values the stability and execution that Cook delivered.
Ternus’s background suggests continuity rather than rupture, yet his success will depend on whether he can turn Apple’s resources into a clearer product vision.

stories.title: OpenAI faces criminal probe over role of ChatGPT in shooting
stories.summary: OpenAI is under criminal investigation over whether ChatGPT played a role in a shooting at Florida State University, a case that intensifies scrutiny of AI safety and liability.
stories.source_signal_title: OpenAI faces criminal probe over role of ChatGPT in shooting
body: OpenAI is under criminal investigation over whether ChatGPT played a role in a shooting at Florida State University, intensifying scrutiny of how AI tools may be used in real-world violence.
The company has said it is not responsible for the attack, but the probe raises difficult questions about liability, safety and the limits of chatbot design.

It also comes at a moment when governments are already under pressure to decide how much responsibility AI firms should bear for harmful uses of their systems, especially if users exploit them in ways developers did not intend.
For OpenAI, the case is likely to deepen concerns about reputational risk and legal exposure just as it tries to position its technology as useful and broadly safe.
What investigators conclude could shape the wider debate over whether AI firms need tougher oversight, clearer safeguards and more explicit rules on accountability.

stories.title: Meta to track workers' clicks and keystrokes to train AI
stories.summary: Meta plans to use workplace activity data, including workers’ clicks and keystrokes, to train its AI models, sharpening the debate over consent, surveillance and productivity inside the company.
stories.source_signal_title: Meta to track workers' clicks and keystrokes to train AI
body: Meta plans to use workplace activity data, including workers’ clicks and keystrokes, to train its AI systems, according to the BBC’s report.
The proposal has sharpened debate inside and outside the company about consent, surveillance and the boundaries of productivity monitoring in the workplace.
Supporters may argue that internal data can help improve AI tools and make them more useful; critics will see a worrying expansion of corporate oversight into ordinary day-to-day behaviour.
The issue matters beyond Meta because it points to a broader trend in which companies increasingly treat employee activity as raw material for artificial intelligence.
That raises legal and ethical questions about what staff are told, what they can opt out of, and how such data is kept secure once it is collected.

stories.title: UK inflation rate rises to 3.3% in March after Iran war pushes up fuel prices
stories.summary: The UK’s March inflation rate has risen to 3.3% as the Iran war pushed up fuel prices, the first official sign of how the conflict is feeding into household costs.
stories.source_signal_title: UK inflation rate rises to 3.3% in March after Iran war pushes up fuel prices
body: UK inflation rose to 3.3% in March after the Iran war pushed fuel prices higher, giving the first official reading of how the conflict is affecting household budgets.
The increase matters because fuel feeds into transport, food distribution and air fares, meaning the effect can spread well beyond the petrol station.
For policymakers, the numbers add to a difficult cost-of-living picture: inflation is no longer easing cleanly, and external shocks are making it harder to predict the path ahead.
The March figure provides a snapshot rather than the full story, and more will depend on whether energy markets stabilise or remain volatile in the weeks ahead.
If the war continues to unsettle oil and shipping markets, British consumers may feel the pressure again before long.

stories.title: Starmer sends 'chill' through civil service, its union boss says
stories.summary: Keir Starmer’s dismissal of Sir Olly Robbins has prompted accusations from the civil service union that a chill has settled over Whitehall, deepening the political fallout from the Mandelson vetting row.
stories.source_signal_title: Starmer sends 'chill' through civil service, its union boss says
body: Keir Starmer’s dismissal of Sir Olly Robbins has drawn accusations from the civil service union that a chill has settled over Whitehall.
The row follows the removal of the Foreign Office’s lead civil servant in the wake of the Mandelson vetting dispute, which has become a fresh source of tension between ministers and officials.
The complaint matters because the civil service depends on a sense that staff can advise ministers openly, even when political decisions are contentious or embarrassing.
Union leaders fear that the episode will make officials more cautious at a time when government is already under pressure to show competence and control.
The longer the dispute continues, the more it may deepen mistrust between ministers and the permanent administration they rely on to carry out policy.

stories.title: Bird flu vaccine trial against potential pandemic strain begins
stories.summary: A bird flu vaccine trial has begun in the UK against the H5N1 strain, which has not yet spread between humans but has caused severe infections in bird populations worldwide.
stories.source_signal_title: Bird flu vaccine trial against potential pandemic strain begins
body: A bird flu vaccine trial has begun in the UK against the H5N1 strain, which has so far not spread between humans.

The trial matters because H5N1 has caused severe and sometimes devastating infections in bird populations worldwide, and scientists are trying to stay ahead of the possibility that it could evolve into a greater human threat.
Starting a trial now is part of the broader effort to prepare for outbreaks before they become emergencies, rather than waiting for the virus to adapt further.
At present there is no evidence of sustained human-to-human spread, but the global spread among birds keeps the issue on public-health watchlists.
If the vaccine proves promising, it could become one of the tools used to reduce the risk of a future pandemic strain.

stories.title: Hundreds of jobs to be created by £50m defence sector deal, officials say
stories.summary: Officials say a £50m defence sector deal will create hundreds of jobs, with Defence Minister Luke Pollard due to visit Belfast as part of the launch.
stories.source_signal_title: Hundreds of jobs to be created by £50m defence sector deal, officials say
body: Officials say a £50m defence sector deal will create hundreds of jobs, with the package being launched as Defence Minister Luke Pollard visits Belfast.
The announcement matters because defence spending is being used not only to strengthen security but also to support industrial work and regional employment.
For places seeking investment, such deals can bring new contracts, apprenticeships and supply-chain opportunities, though the long-term benefits depend on how much of the work stays local.
The wider context is a Europe that is rearming and rethinking defence capacity, which has increased interest in domestic manufacturing and resilience.
The key question now is whether the promised jobs arrive quickly and whether the deal can deliver sustained work beyond the initial launch.

stories.title: Anthropic investigating claim of unauthorised access to Mythos AI tool
stories.summary: Anthropic says it is investigating claims of unauthorised access to Mythos, its AI system that it has described as too dangerous to release publicly because of its hacking capabilities.
stories.source_signal_title: Anthropic investigating claim of unauthorised access to Mythos AI tool
body: Anthropic says it is investigating claims of unauthorised access to Mythos, its AI system that it has described as too dangerous to release publicly because of its hacking capabilities.
The allegation matters because it involves a tool that the company itself has said should not be widely deployed, making security around the model especially sensitive.
If the claim is confirmed, it could intensify concerns about how advanced AI systems are controlled, who can reach them and what risks emerge if they are misused.
The episode also adds to a wider debate in the AI industry over whether some capabilities are becoming too powerful to manage safely with current safeguards.
Anthropic has not said how serious the alleged access was, and that uncertainty will shape attention in the days ahead.

stories.title: UK watchdog to investigate Telegram over alleged child sexual abuse material
stories.summary: Ofcom has launched an investigation into Telegram after receiving evidence that alleged child sexual abuse material was being shared on the messaging platform.
stories.source_signal_title: UK watchdog to investigate Telegram over alleged child sexual abuse material
body: Ofcom has launched an investigation into Telegram after receiving evidence that alleged child sexual abuse material was being shared on the messaging platform.
The case is significant because it tests how far the UK’s Online Safety Act can force large platforms to prevent the spread of illegal content, even when they operate at global scale.

Telegram has long been criticised for the difficulty of moderating content on its service, and any finding against it could have wider implications for messaging apps that rely on encrypted or semi-private spaces.

The regulator’s inquiry follows evidence from the Canadian Centre for Child Protection, but the outcome is not yet known and Telegram has said it categorically denies the accusations in related reporting.
What happens next will help determine whether the new legal framework can produce meaningful enforcement rather than simply more warnings.

stories.title: Could a digital twin make you into a 'superworker'?
stories.summary: The spread of digital twins in workplaces is being sold as a productivity leap, but the idea raises legal and ethical questions about surveillance, labour rights and control.
stories.source_signal_title: Could a digital twin make you into a 'superworker'?
body: Digital twins are being promoted as a way to make workers more productive, but the idea also raises uncomfortable questions about surveillance and control.

In principle, a digital copy of a worker’s patterns and behaviour could help organisations plan tasks more efficiently or automate repetitive decisions; in practice, it may also give employers a deeper view of how staff behave.

That matters because workplace technology often arrives as a productivity tool first and a labour issue second, leaving questions about consent, ownership of data and employee rights to be settled later.
If firms want to use such systems at scale, they will have to confront the possibility that they are creating not just a new tool, but a new legal minefield.

stories.title: Why are respected film-makers suddenly embracing AI?
stories.summary: Steven Soderbergh and other prominent film-makers are experimenting with AI in production, signalling a broader shift in the film industry even as concerns about authorship and labour persist.
stories.source_signal_title: Why are respected film-makers suddenly embracing AI?
body: A growing number of respected film-makers, including Steven Soderbergh, are experimenting with AI in production, signalling a shift in how parts of the industry think about the technology.
The trend matters because film-makers have often been among the strongest critics of generative AI, especially where it touches authorship, labour and the work of actors and writers.
Yet some directors now appear willing to use AI as another creative tool, whether for imagery, visual effects or idea generation, suggesting the debate is moving from principle to practice.
That does not settle the ethical concerns: questions remain over training data, ownership and whether AI will weaken the bargaining position of creative workers.
What happens next will depend on whether these experiments stay limited or become a normal part of the production process.

stories.title: Cardiff takeover deal with Ospreys owners collapses
stories.summary: The Welsh Rugby Union says negotiations for the Ospreys owners to buy Cardiff have fallen through, leaving Cardiff’s future ownership unresolved.
stories.source_signal_title: Cardiff takeover deal with Ospreys owners collapses
body: The Welsh Rugby Union says negotiations for the Ospreys owners to buy Cardiff have collapsed, leaving the club’s future ownership unresolved.
The breakdown matters because Cardiff’s long-term stability has been a recurring issue, and any change in ownership is likely to affect recruitment, finances and planning on and off the field.
For supporters, the immediate concern is uncertainty: without a deal, there is no clear path to settling the club’s structure or reassuring fans about what comes next.
The WRU has not said what alternative options are under discussion, so the position remains fluid.
This leaves Cardiff in a familiar limbo, with the club’s future once again dependent on negotiations away from the pitch.

stories.title: Rangers captain Tavernier to leave in summer after 11 years
stories.summary: James Tavernier says he will leave Rangers at the end of the season after 11 years at Ibrox, a significant change for the Scottish club.
stories.source_signal_title: Rangers captain Tavernier to leave in summer after 11 years
body: Rangers captain James Tavernier will leave Ibrox at the end of the season after 11 years at the club.
His departure marks a significant change for Rangers, where Tavernier has been one of the most recognisable figures of the modern era and a long-serving leader on and off the pitch.
Such exits matter not only for squad planning but also for dressing-room continuity, especially at a club where expectations remain high every season.
The next question is how Rangers replace both his experience and his leadership, and whether they do so through an internal successor or the transfer market.
For now, the club is facing another summer of transition.

stories.title: Real Madrid's Tchouameni on Man Utd radar
stories.summary: Real Madrid midfielder Aurélien Tchouaméni is among the players Manchester United are looking at as they target a central midfield signing this summer.
stories.source_signal_title: Real Madrid's Tchouameni on Man Utd radar
body: Manchester United are looking at Real Madrid midfielder Aurélien Tchouaméni as they plan for a central midfield signing this summer.
The interest matters because United’s midfield has been a recurring area of concern, and a player of Tchouaméni’s profile would signal a serious attempt to add quality and control.
Real Madrid are unlikely to make any deal straightforward, so this is very much one name on a list rather than a completed pursuit.
Even so, the link offers an early indication of the kind of player United may target: proven at the top level, physically strong and capable of strengthening the spine of the side.
As ever with summer transfer talk, the main uncertainty is whether interest becomes a genuine bid.

stories.title: Spain defender Batlle close to agreeing Arsenal move
stories.summary: Spain defender Ona Batlle is close to agreeing a summer move to Arsenal as a free agent, a potentially important addition for the women’s side.
stories.source_signal_title: Spain defender Batlle close to agreeing Arsenal move
body: Spain defender Ona Batlle is close to agreeing a move to Arsenal as a free agent in the summer.
The possible signing would be an important addition for Arsenal’s women’s side, giving them experience and quality in a position where elite depth matters over a long season.
Because Batlle would arrive on a free transfer, the move could also represent strong value if the deal is completed.
The broader significance is that top women’s clubs are now competing aggressively for proven international players, and Arsenal remain intent on staying among Europe’s leading sides.
The deal is not yet final, but it appears to be moving in the right direction.
